title_zh: 安娜·门迪塔：以“身体”为媒介的艺术家

paragraphs.en: Decades after her death, her bold innovations are finally coming into focus.
paragraphs.zh: 在她去世多年后，她大胆的创新终于逐渐被人看见。

paragraphs.en: Before Ana Mendieta, the Cuban American artist, became a fellow of the American Academy in Rome, in 1983, she’d never had a studio of her own. Five years earlier, as an ambitious twenty-nine-year-old, she’d moved to New York City in the depths of winter, and soon rented a poky, dark apartment on the ground floor of a building in Greenwich Village. “Desolate” was how she described the place. She scavenged planks of wood for shelves and a table, and she carved out a workspace for herself beneath a loft bed, which had a mattress that she’d dragged in from the street.
paragraphs.zh: 在成为罗马美国学院会士之前，古巴裔美国艺术家安娜·门迪塔从未拥有过属于自己的工作室。五年前，她在万般不易中怀着抱负来到纽约，还是个二十九岁的年轻女性。她很快就在格林威治村的一栋楼房一层租下了一间狭窄阴暗的公寓。她把那里形容为“荒凉”。她到处拾来木板做成书架和桌子，并在一张阁楼式床铺下开辟出自己的工作空间，那张床上有一张她从街上拽回来的床垫。

paragraphs.en: The limitations of the space didn’t matter so much, though, given the artistic path that Mendieta was forging. She had originally seen herself as a painter, but she’d more or less relinquished the medium by 1972, the year she finished a graduate degree in the subject at the University of Iowa. “My paintings were not real enough for what I wanted the image to convey,” she once explained. “And by real I mean I wanted my images to have power, to be magic.” Instead of creating conventional canvases or sculptures—works that needed wall or floor space to be made and displayed—Mendieta had developed an artistic practice that might combine performance, land art, and an instrumental use of her own body. In 1982, Mendieta had staged one such work, “Body Tracks,” at Franklin Furnace, a multidisciplinary venue in what was then the cast-iron wilderness of Tribeca. A small audience assembled in a room where three large blank sheets of paper had been affixed to a wall. On the floor was a basin filled with a viscous liquid, tempera paint mixed with animal blood that was likely procured from a slaughterhouse. Then Mendieta—an energetic and diminutive woman, just five feet tall, if that—walked in, to the accompaniment of a drumbeat, dressed in a baggy white ensemble. She drenched her hands and arms in the mixture, reached to the top of one piece of paper, then forcefully smeared her limbs down the surface to make two bloody tracks, ending up on her knees on the floor. Mendieta performed this action twice more before absenting herself, leaving the audience to contemplate the visceral imprints she left behind.
paragraphs.zh: 不过，鉴于门迪塔正在开创的艺术道路，空间的局促并不算什么。她起初将自己定位为一名画家，但到 1972 年基本放弃了这种媒介；那一年她在爱荷华大学完成了绘画研究生学位。她曾经解释说：“我的绘画还不够真实，无法承载我想要表达的图像。”她所说的“真实”，是指她想要图像有力量、具有魔力。门迪塔没有去创作传统意义上的画布作品或雕塑——这种作品需要靠墙面或地面来完成和展示——而是形成了把表演、地景艺术以及对自身身体的工具化运用结合起来的艺术实践。1982 年，她在法兰克林·弗恩奇（Franklin Furnace）表演过一件这样的作品《Body Tracks》。那是当时特里贝卡一片铸铁时代荒凉景观中的一个跨媒介表演空间。一个小型观众群聚在一间房间里，墙上贴着三张大幅白色纸张。地板上放着一个浅盆，里面装着黏稠的液体——温彩颜料与动物血混合而成，血液很可能来自屠宰场。随后，门迪塔走了进来，身着宽松白色服装，随着鼓点进入，会场里的人都能感受到她的力量与矫健。她把双手和双臂浸入那种混合液中，抓着一张纸的上沿，用力将手臂和身体沿着纸面拖过，留下一道两道血迹，最后跪伏在地。她又重复了两次这一动作，然后退场，留下观众面对她留下的触感强烈的印痕。

paragraphs.en: In Rome, however, Mendieta had space and light, and—with a studio perched over the academy’s lovely, pine-filled gardens atop the Janiculum Hill—immediate access to the natural world. She could also enjoy new urban pleasures: she screeched around the city in a Volkswagen while yelling through her rolled-down window in demotic Italian; as a native Spanish speaker, the language came easily to her. Friends observed that Mendieta—who had been born in Cuba but, at the age of twelve, was sent to live in exile in the United States—had found in Rome a warm, sociable culture that was reassuringly familiar. Mendieta was vivacious, fast-talking, intense; she got into arguments and very loudly held her ground. In Italy, she could be like this without being stereotyped as a hotheaded Latina, as she often had been in America.
paragraphs.zh: 但在罗马，门迪塔终于有了空间和光线；更重要的是，她拥有一间俯瞰学院上方、松树点缀的漂亮花园、位于雅尼库勒姆山上的工作室——因此能立即接近自然世界。她也开始享受新的城市乐趣：她坐在一辆大众汽车里在城里呼啸穿梭，车窗摇下后，用带有地方口语色彩的意大利语高声喊叫；而作为一名以西班牙语为母语的人来说，这门语言对她来说并不困难。朋友们观察到，门迪塔出生于古巴，但十二岁时被送往美国流亡，在罗马她找到了一个温暖、热情、而且令人安心熟悉的文化氛围。门迪塔活泼、谈吐快捷而激烈、情感强烈；她会争辩，而且会大声坚持自己的立场。在意大利，她可以毫不顾忌地这样做，而不会像在美国那样被贴上“冲动拉丁裔女性”的刻板标签。

paragraphs.en: Her experience in Rome was so liberating that her artistic practice opened up, too. Mendieta enlisted a gardener at the academy to help her haul a fallen plane tree into her studio. She obtained other trees, then stripped their bark and burned abstracted silhouettes of human figures into the exposed grain, using gunpowder that she kept stashed in a cookie tin. Ida Panicelli, a curator who got to know Mendieta in Rome, and who would go on to serve as the editor of Artforum in the late eighties and early nineties, recalled recently, “She could finally produce things. Her work was so ephemeral before.”
paragraphs.zh: 她在罗马的经历让她感到前所未有的解放，因而她的创作也随之打开了新的可能。门迪塔在学院里找来一名园丁，帮她把一棵倒下的梧桐树搬进工作室。她又拿到了其他树木，然后剥去树皮，用她藏在曲奇饼盒里的黑色火药，在露出的年轮上烧出抽象的人体轮廓。后来在罗马与她相识、并在八十年代末到九十年代初担任《Artforum》编辑的馆长伊达·潘西利（Ida Panicelli）最近回忆道：“她终于能够真正开始创作了。她以前的作品太短暂了。”

paragraphs.en: Four forms that Mendieta made from those tree trunks in Rome make up a freestanding work that’s catalogued as “La Jungla (Totem Grove).” The series will be on display, starting this summer, at the Tate Modern, in London, as part of a wide-ranging exhibition of the artist’s work. The show, one of several recent and forthcoming exhibitions dedicated to Mendieta’s œuvre, will also include some of the works for which she is best known, from her years-long “Silueta Series.” In these pieces, made between 1973 and 1980, Mendieta used the measurements or the outline of her body to mark the ground, or placed herself in juxtaposition with trees, flowers, and other aspects of the natural world. These “earth-body” works, as she often called them, were documented in photographs or on film. Among them is perhaps Mendieta’s most recognizable work, “Imágen de Yágul,” a color photograph that she made in Oaxaca, Mexico, in 1973. In the image, she poses nude within the rocky interior of an ancient Mesoamerican tomb, with sprays of white blossoms rising from between her legs and sandwiched between her arms and her torso, her face hidden by the flowers. The vitality of her body is apparent even as she lies still in a space built for burial; her hands are poised on her hips, and her feet appear to be raised, hovering slightly above the dark shadow of the earth.
paragraphs.zh: 门迪塔在罗马用这些树干制作的四种形式构成了一件立式作品，名为《La Jungla（Totem Grove）》，也就是《丛林（图腾林）》。这组作品将从今年夏天开始在伦敦泰特现代美术馆展出，作为该艺术家高覆盖度展览的一部分。该展览是近来以及即将推出的多场门迪塔专题展之一，也将展出一些她最知名的作品，来自她持续多年创作的《Silueta系列》。在这批作品中（创作于1973年至1980年），门迪塔使用了自己身体的尺寸或轮廓去标记地面，或者让自己与树木、鲜花及自然界其他元素并置。她常把这类“地体”作品用胶片或照片记录下来。其中也许是最具辨识度的作品之一，是1973年在墨西哥瓦哈卡（Oaxaca）拍摄的彩色照片《Imágen de Yágul》。照片中，她裸露身体地站在一座古代中美洲墓葬的岩石内部，白色花朵从她腿间绽放、夹在她的手臂与躯干之间，并遮住她的脸。尽管她躺在为埋葬而建的空间中静止不动，她的生命力却十分明显；她的手搭在臀部，双脚似乎抬起，微微悬在大地黑色阴影之上。

paragraphs.en: Mendieta’s name recognition, at least to the broad museumgoing public, is not comparable to that of, say, Frida Kahlo or Tracey Emin, both of whom have also been celebrated with Tate shows this year. But Mendieta’s art has been increasingly embraced in recent years, especially by young women, who recognize her as a kind of contemporary, even if her work was made half a century ago. Katy Hessel, the author of “The Story of Art Without Men,” a female-centered survey, calls Mendieta “one of the most important artists of the twentieth century.” The essayist and critic Olivia Laing, in her book “Everybody,” from 2021, places Mendieta in the context of the second-wave feminism of Andrea Dworkin, who wrote with incendiary fury about the violence of men toward women, and of Angela Carter, whose novels and short stories vividly reinterpreted myths and fairy tales with a feminine awareness of erotic danger. Mendieta’s work, Laing writes, suggests “the certainty of destruction, and the certainty too of abiding, resisting.” Laing also observed that Mendieta “used violent material in ways that feel full of liberating possibility, but that doesn’t mean she herself was out of harm’s way.”
paragraphs.zh: 就普通观众而言，门迪塔的知名度并不及弗里达·卡罗（Frida Kahlo）或特雷西·艾敏（Tracey Emin）——这两位艺术家今年也都在泰特美术馆举办了展览——也就不足为奇。如今，门迪塔的艺术却在近几年被越来越多的人，尤其是年轻女性所拥抱。她们把门迪塔视作当代女性艺术的一种代表，尽管她的作品诞生于半个世纪前。女性主义导向、作家凯蒂·赫塞尔（Katy Hessel）在《没有男人的艺术史》（The Story of Art Without Men）中称门迪塔是“二十世纪最重要的艺术家之一”。《Everyone》一书作者、批评家奥利维亚·莱英（Olivia Laing）在2021年的著作里，把门迪塔放进了安德烈亚·德沃金（Andrea Dworkin）所代表的第二波女性主义语境。德沃金曾以“燃烧般的愤怒”控诉男性对女性的暴力；安吉拉·卡特（Angela Carter）的小说和短篇故事也以女性意识重释神话与童话中的性感危险。莱英写道，门迪塔的作品暗示着“毁灭的必然性，也有坚持与抗拒的必然性”。她还指出，门迪塔“以一种看似充满解放可能性的方式使用暴力材料，但这并不意味着她本人就安全无虞”。

paragraphs.en: The certainty of destruction was an essential dimension of Mendieta’s art. The outline of a body that Mendieta stamped into river mud would inevitably be eroded by weather and water; the feathers that she stuck to her skin could disperse as she ran naked along a beach. Like all art that incorporates elements of performance and temporality, Mendieta’s is not always easy to access or appreciate, and the films she made documenting her work—the digitization of which has been painstakingly overseen by her niece Raquel Cecilia Mendieta, who now heads the Ana Mendieta estate—can’t fully capture the moments of execution. There are few artists whose pieces more keenly make a viewer wish that aroma could be conveyed by digital reproduction: the vegetal scent of mud, the heady perfume of flowers, the iron reek of blood. Much of Mendieta’s work has a striking visual effect at its core: in “Untitled (Glass on Body Imprints),” a series from the early seventies, photographs capture her through a sheet of glass onto which she has pressed her face and body, smearing her nose or cheek or mouth into a distorted fleshy mass—a gesture that the British artist Jenny Saville would echo some twenty years later, in her collaborations with the photographer Glen Luchford. Other Mendieta works are more private, even secretive, including the “Rupestrian Sculptures,” made in the early eighties, in which Mendieta carved figures into the walls of limestone grottoes in Cuba. Mendieta was active at a time when female artists, especially female artists of color, had so much less currency than their male counterparts that being overlooked, however much it was resented, offered its own kind of freedom. It’s impossible to view Mendieta’s output and not think about the physical stamina and cultural daring that she must have had—not only to make art from her exposed body but also to hide herself away and make work that might only ever please or satisfy herself.
paragraphs.zh: “毁灭的必然性”是门迪塔艺术的核心维度。她在河泥中按印人体轮廓的动作，必然会被风化与水流侵蚀；她贴在皮肤上的羽毛，在她赤裸着沿海滩奔跑时也会被吹散。和所有融合了表演与时间特性的艺术一样，门迪塔的作品并不总是容易被观看和理解，而她用来记录创作过程的电影——由她的侄女拉凯尔·塞西莉亚·门迪塔（Raquel Cecilia Mendieta）悉心主导数字化，她现任Ana Mendieta艺术遗产负责人——也无法完整重现创作瞬间。有些艺术家中，门迪塔的作品最能让人希望数字再现也能传递气味：泥土的植物气息、花香的醉人甜润、血液的铁锈气息。门迪塔许多作品都以强烈的视觉效果为核心。例如在早期七十年代的系列《Untitled (Glass on Body Imprints)（无题：身体印记·玻璃）》中，照片从一张玻璃纸上拍下她的面孔与身体，她把鼻子、脸颊或嘴巴压在玻璃上，留下一团模糊扭曲的肉感印迹；二十年后，英国艺术家珍妮·萨维尔（Jenny Saville）在与摄影师格伦·卢切福德（Glen Luchford）的合作中也会回应这一动作。门迪塔的另一些作品更为私密，甚至带有隐秘性，包括八十年代初的“Rupestrian Sculptures（露天岩雕）”：她在古巴石灰岩洞穴的岩壁上雕刻人物形象。门迪塔活跃于一个女性艺术家，特别是有色女性艺术家，远不及男性同行受重视的时期；因此，被忽视虽然令人怨恨，却也赋予了一种不同的自由。面对门迪塔的全部创作，很难不想到她必须具备怎样的体力和文化勇气——不仅要以裸露之身完成创作，还要将自己藏起来，去做一种也许只会取悦或满足她自己的作品。

paragraphs.en: The Tate Modern show will be arranged thematically, with each of the sections representing a facet of the natural world explored in Mendieta’s work. The first section, called the Cave, is based on pieces made in those grottoes in Cuba, and features what might be called neo-Neolithic images—figures resembling fertility goddesses. Other sections are named the Grove, the River, the Sacred Grounds. The museum also plans to re-stage some of Mendieta’s ephemeral works. Staffers at the Tate Modern have been growing a crop of sphagnum moss in order to re-create a living sculpture of wire mesh and climbing plants. The piece, called “Árbol de la Vida / Mother of All Things,” will be assembled against a tree outside the building. (These works will be executed in part by Raquel Cecilia Mendieta, who will reconstruct them from images in cases when the artist left no written instructions.) The curators have also selected several paintings that Mendieta worked on as a student; museumgoers can assess the presence or absence of magic in them. Valentine Umansky, one of the show’s curators, explained to me, “It’s not really organized in a way that follows the axis of her life, because, in a way, all the themes loop.”
paragraphs.zh: 泰特现代美术馆的展览将按主题布展，每个展区都代表着门蒂塔作品中自然世界的一个侧面。第一单元名为“洞穴”，根据她在古巴石洞里创作的作品展开，展出的是可被称作“新新石器时代”图像的作品——类似生育女神的形象。其他单元分别命名为“林地”“河流”“圣地”。美术馆还计划复排门蒂塔的一些短暂性作品。泰特现代的工作人员正在培育一种藓类植物，以便重现一个由金属网和攀缘植物组成的“活体雕塑”。该作品名为《生命之树/万物之母》，将被安装在大楼外的一棵树上。 （当艺术家没有留下书面说明时，这些作品将由拉克尔·塞西利亚·门蒂塔部分依照影像进行重构。）策展人还挑选了门蒂塔在学生时期创作的多幅绘画；观众可据此评判其作品中是否存在“魔性”成分。展览策展人之一瓦伦廷·乌曼斯基对我说：“它并没有真正按照她人生的时间轴来组织，因为在某种意义上，所有主题都是循环的。”

paragraphs.en: The thematic presentation helps underscore the idea that Mendieta’s work prefigured the environmental concerns that have lately become widespread among artists. (As the critic Ben Davis recently noted, this year’s Venice Biennale includes “unbelievable amounts of work about plants, and water, and farming.”) Umansky suggested that younger audiences may find in Mendieta’s art an exploration of the malleability of identity. She was playing with the concept of gender as early as 1972. That year, in “Untitled (Facial Hair Transplants),” which was preserved in a series of photographs, Mendieta and a friend, the poet Morty Sklar, stood side by side in front of a mirror; as Sklar shaved his beard off, he handed clumps of hair to Mendieta, who applied them to her own face with glue, resulting in a realistic if unruly chin-strap. Umansky told me that by the final section of the show, called the Threshold, a visitor should have a sense of Mendieta as an artist of transformation: “transforming into a dog, transforming into a tree, transforming into a bird, transforming by changing genders, transforming her face—with this idea of this energy or life force that allows this transformation, from death to rebirth, and also from one state to another, one element to another.” Umansky went on, “The show really ends with this idea, which is also something that I think contemporary audiences can resonate to: We can reinvent ourselves. Nothing ends. We constantly transform. And all that we touch changes us.”
paragraphs.zh: 这种主题化呈现强化了这样一个观念：门蒂塔的创作预示了当代艺术家中日益普遍的环境议题。正如评论家本·戴维斯最近指出的，今年威尼斯双年展上出现了“难以置信数量”的关于植物、水以及农业的作品。乌曼斯基认为，年轻观众或许会在门蒂塔的艺术里看到对身份可塑性的探索。她早在1972年就开始玩味性别这一概念。那一年，在《无题（面部毛发移植）》中——这件作品被一组照片保存下来——门蒂塔与一位朋友、诗人莫提·斯克拉尔并肩站在镜前；当斯克拉尔剃掉胡须时，他把一团团胡须递给门蒂塔，后者用胶水贴在自己的脸上，形成了一副逼真却略显散乱的络腮胡。乌曼斯基告诉我，到了展览最后一单元“门槛”时，观众应当会对门蒂塔有一种“变化中的艺术家”的印象：“变成一条狗，变成一棵树，变成一只鸟，通过改变性别进行转变，通过改变面貌进行转变——带着这样一种观念：有一种能量或生命力使这些转化成为可能，从死亡走向重生，也从一种状态走向另一种状态，从一种元素走向另一种元素。”乌曼斯基接着说：“展览真正以这个观念作为结尾，我认为当代观众也能与之产生共鸣：我们可以重塑自己。没有什么是终点。我们不断地在变化。我们所接触的一切也在改变我们。”

paragraphs.en: Mendieta’s first transformation was forced upon her, when she was on the cusp of adolescence. Although in New York City she lived the frugal existence of an impoverished artist—she paid her rent, in part, by working as a waitress at Food, the artists’ canteen in SoHo—she was born, in 1948, into Cuba’s social and economic élite. One of her great-grandfathers, Carlos María de Rojas, had been a hero of the 1895 War of Independence, and a great-uncle, Carlos Mendieta, had briefly served as President of Cuba, in the nineteen-thirties. Her father, Ignacio Mendieta, was a prominent lawyer, and her mother, Raquel, was a chemistry teacher. Ana and her sister, Raquelin, who was two years older, grew up with servants at the family’s homes, in Havana and on seaside properties outside the city.
paragraphs.zh: 门蒂塔最初的“转变”是被迫发生的，那时她正处于青春期的门槛上。尽管在纽约市，她过着一位贫困艺术家的节俭生活——她靠在SoHo艺术家食堂Food做服务员支付部分房租——她却出生于1948年出身于古巴的社会与经济精英阶层。她的曾祖父之一卡洛斯·马里亚·德·罗哈斯是1895年独立战争的英雄，而一位曾叔父卡洛斯·门蒂塔在上世纪三十年代曾短暂担任古巴总统。她的父亲伊格纳西奥·门蒂塔是知名律师，母亲拉克尔是化学教师。安娜和比她大两岁的姐姐拉克琳在哈瓦那及该市外的滨海地产中与仆役一起长大。

paragraphs.en: This privileged childhood was ruptured in 1960, a year after the Cuban Revolution ended. Mendieta’s father, who was part of a group that had helped the C.I.A. work against the rise of Fidel Castro, became concerned for his and his family’s safety, and Raquelin and Ana were among some fourteen thousand Cuban children who were sent to the United States as part of a program known as Operation Peter Pan. (Ignacio’s fears were justified: he was imprisoned by the Castro regime, and wasn’t released until the late seventies.) The sisters spent several weeks in a refugee camp in Florida, and were then dispatched to an orphanage in Dubuque, Iowa. Locals became their foster parents, treating them with differing degrees of kindness. “I don’t care who you were in Cuba—you’re nobody in this country!” one foster parent reportedly told them. It would be five years before the sisters were reunited with their mother and their younger brother, after they also relocated to the U.S.
paragraphs.zh: 这种优越的童年在1960年被打断，那一年是古巴革命结束后的一年。门蒂塔的父亲曾参与一个帮助美国中情局反对菲德尔·卡斯特罗上台的组织，因此担心自己和家人的安全，拉克琳和安娜便与约一万四千名古巴儿童一起，作为“彼得·潘行动”（Operation Peter Pan）的一部分被送往美国。 （伊格纳西奥的担忧是有道理的：他曾被卡斯特罗政权监禁，直到七十年代末才获释。）姐妹俩在佛罗里达的一个难民营待了数周，随后被送往爱荷华州得比克的孤儿院。当地主人家收留她们为寄养家庭，善意的程度各不相同。有一位寄养父母 reportedly 对她们说过：“我不在乎你在古巴是什么身份，在这个国家你算不了什么！”直到姐妹俩随后也搬到美国，她们与母亲和更小的弟弟团聚前，还经历了整整五年的时间。

paragraphs.en: Despite receiving no encouragement from her high-school art teacher, who failed to identify any talent in her first sculptures, Mendieta enrolled at the University of Iowa to study art. After graduating, she enrolled in a master’s program at Iowa and taught art at an elementary school. Her practice eventually grew more experimental under the influence of a professor named Hans Breder, who also became her romantic partner for several years. Breder had founded an interdisciplinary “Intermedia” program at the university; in a statement from 1971, he and two other artists encouraged others to see art “as an indispensable force in the life of any human culture, away from the inanimate investment hanging on the wall or trickling out of the background stereo.” Breder, who was thirteen years Mendieta’s senior, was well connected in avant-garde circles, and he invited some of his forward-thinking friends to visit Iowa. One was Vito Acconci, whose work included “Following Piece,” in which he surreptitiously trailed pedestrians around New York City.
paragraphs.zh: 尽管高中美术老师对她毫无鼓励——那位老师未能在她的第一批雕塑中发现任何天赋——门迪耶塔仍然进入了爱荷华大学学习艺术。毕业后，她在爱荷华大学攻读硕士课程，并在一所小学任教。她的创作后来在一位名叫汉斯·布勒德（Hans Breder）的教授影响下逐渐走向更具实验性，这位教授在之后的几年里也成为了她的恋人。布勒德在该大学创立了一个跨媒介的“Intermedia”课程；在1971年的一份声明中，他和另外两位艺术家鼓励人们将艺术视为“任何人类文化生活中不可或缺的一种力量，而不是挂在墙上、毫无生气的装饰品，也不是从背景立体声中悄然流出的背景音”。比门迪耶塔大13岁的布勒德在前卫圈层里人脉广泛，他邀请了一些前瞻性的朋友来爱荷华大学走访。其中一位是维托·阿孔奇（Vito Acconci），他的作品《跟随片段》（Following Piece）包含他在纽约市内悄悄尾随行人的行为。

paragraphs.en: Mendieta began staging her own site-specific projects, including several works that refer powerfully to sexual violence. In one work, made in response to the brutal assault and murder of another female student, she went into her apartment and stripped from the waist down, smeared herself in animal blood, and bent over a table, leaving her door ajar so that others could enter and confront the scene of an imagined crime. For “Moffitt Building Piece,” Mendieta poured blood on the sidewalk outside an apartment building so that it seeped to the edge of a surrounding paving stone, forming a crimson puddle. Mendieta then documented the reactions of passersby—a parade of the oblivious and the curious, with none trying to intervene in the grisly scene.
paragraphs.zh: 门迪耶塔开始布置她自己的场域特定作品，其中有若干作品强有力地指向性暴力。在一件作品中，她回应另一名女性学生遭受的残忍袭击与谋杀：她走进公寓，脱去下半身衣物，用动物血给自己涂抹，并俯身趴在桌前，留下半掩的门，让旁人得以进入并面对一场想象中的犯罪现场。对于《莫菲特大楼作品》（Moffitt Building Piece），门迪耶塔在一栋公寓楼外的人行道上倒了血，使其渗入周围石板边缘，形成一滩鲜红的水迹。随后，门迪耶塔记录了路人的反应——一个由茫然者与好奇者组成的队列，没人尝试干预那场骇人的场景。

paragraphs.en: There is a confrontational anger in these works. Mendieta described one such piece as a “reaction against the idea of violence against women,” and feminist critics later came to regard them as prescient commentaries on the vulnerability of women’s bodies. (She was not the only female artist in the nineteen-seventies to address the issue of rape, a subject that art has traditionally stylized more than condemned; in 1977, Suzanne Lacy presented a three-week-long performance piece in Los Angeles in which she stamped reported rapes on a map each day.) During this formative period, blood became one of Mendieta’s primary media. “Sweating Blood,” which was recorded with a Super 8 camera, shows Mendieta’s face in closeup. She is radiant, which makes it all the more disturbing when syrupy rivulets start trickling down her unblemished, dispassionate visage. The result is a self-portrait suggestive of images of Christ and of saintly relics—central motifs in Catholicism, the religion in which Mendieta was raised. The ways Mendieta used blood were various, and not always straightforwardly suggestive of harm. She had grown up around members of her family’s household staff who followed the sacrificial rituals of Santería, the Afro-Cuban religion that combines elements of Catholicism with Yoruba practices, in which blood is regarded as a sacred force. In 1972, Mendieta alluded to such practices in “Chicken Movie, Chicken Piece,” for which she stood naked holding a decapitated fowl, her body spattered with its blood.
paragraphs.zh: 这些作品中充满了对抗性的愤怒。门迪耶塔把其中一件作品描述为“反抗对女性暴力这一观念的回应”，而后期的女性主义评论家也把它们看作对女性身体脆弱性的先见性评论。（她并非70年代唯独一位触及强奸议题的女艺术家；艺术长期以来更擅长把这个题材美学化，而非谴责。1977年，苏珊·拉西（Suzanne Lacy）在洛杉矶做过一场为期三周的表演，她每天在地图上标记当天发生的强奸案件。）在这一形成期，血液成为门迪耶塔的主要媒介之一。以超八毫米摄像机拍摄的《出汗的血》（Sweating Blood》）中，可见门迪耶塔的面部特写。她显得容光焕发，这使得当黏稠的血丝缓缓从她无瑕且冷静的面容上滑落时更显诡异。其结果是一幅自画像，联想起基督形象和圣物，这些是天主教的核心母题——门迪耶塔成长于这种宗教传统。门迪耶塔使用血液的方式多样，且并不总是直接指向伤害。她成长时与家庭中的家政成员接触较多，而这些人遵行圣特瑞亚（Santería）的献祭仪式，这种融合天主教与约鲁巴传统的古巴非洲宗教中，血被视为神圣力量。1972年，门迪耶塔在《鸡电影，鸡作品》（Chicken Movie, Chicken Piece）中暗示了这种实践：她赤裸站立，抱着一只被斩首的家禽，身体上溅满其血迹。

paragraphs.en: Mendieta found her most potent theme by representing the female body not as a site of violence but in states of ecstatic fusion with nature. Breder once gave an account of an early gesture by Mendieta in this direction. One afternoon, she and several other graduate students gathered outside his studio. “The smell of freshly cut grass is hanging in the air,” he wrote. “Spontaneously, Mendieta undresses, lies on the lawn, and asks one of the students to cover her body with grass so that it blends into the ground.”
paragraphs.zh: 门迪耶塔最终找到了最有力量的主题，即将女性身体不是塑造成暴力场景，而是以与自然狂喜融合的状态呈现。布勒德曾描述她在这一方向上的一个早期姿态。某个下午，她与几位研究生在他的工作室外聚在一起。“刚剪下的新草味弥漫在空气中，”他写道。“门迪耶塔自发地脱掉衣服，躺在草坪上，并请一位学生用草覆盖她的身体，让她与土地融为一体。”

paragraphs.en: Mendieta began documenting herself lying in streambeds and reclining on rocks. She stood against the trunk of a tree after slathering herself in lumpy mud, so that her body merged visually with the gnarled bark. Such works were often physically demanding to make, but resulted in sublimely beautiful effects. In “Grass Breathing,” from around 1975, she hid herself almost seamlessly beneath a layer of sod on a verdant lawn, causing the ground to rise and fall with the rhythm of her respiration. As her explorations progressed, Mendieta’s process became less literal, and she sometimes used a body-size cutout to stamp her form into the earth. By the time she was working with the tree trunks from Italy, her representation of the human body had been reduced to an abstraction of shapes and geometries. A viewer might not even recognize the form as human.
paragraphs.zh: 门迪塔开始拍摄自己躺在河床里、仰卧在岩石上的样子。她先把自己涂满一层不平整的泥巴，再倚在树干旁边，让身体在视觉上与盘根错节的树皮融合在一起。这样的作品往往在制作上十分费力，但会形成一种庄严而优美的效果。在大约1975年的《Grass Breathing》中，她几乎无缝地躲在一层草皮下，身处一片茂盛的草坪中，使地面随着她的呼吸起伏起落。随着探索的深入，门迪塔的创作过程变得不再直白，她有时会用与人体等大的剪影在泥土地面上“印”下身体形状。到了她开始处理来自意大利的树干时，她对人体的表现已经被简化为形状和几何构成的抽象。观者甚至可能无法认出那是人形。

paragraphs.en: The show at the Tate Modern also emphasizes another of Mendieta’s consistent themes: the sorrow of being exiled from her homeland. She depicted her separation from Cuba not as an abstract loss but as an excruciating physical tear. “I am overwhelmed by the feeling of having been cast out from the womb,” she once said. She viewed her art as “the way I re-establish the bonds that unite me to the Universe.”
paragraphs.zh: 泰特现代美术馆的展览同样强调了门迪塔另一条始终贯穿其创作的主题：被流放离开故土的悲恸。她把自己与古巴分离的经历，不表现为一种抽象的失落，而是近乎撕裂般的身体痛楚。她曾说过：“我被从子宫中被抛出去的感觉让我不胜压抑。” 她把自己的艺术看作“我重新建立我与宇宙之间联系的方式”。

paragraphs.en: Above all, the thematic approach of the Tate Modern exhibition resists the dark pull exerted by Mendieta’s biography. Placing the tree trunks scorched with gunpowder in the second gallery allows them to be enjoyed without the freight of knowing what happened to the artist soon after her return to New York from Rome. The burned trunks, as it turned out, were the last major works Mendieta produced before her sudden, violent death.
paragraphs.zh: 最重要的是，泰特现代美术馆展览的主题取向是在抵抗门迪塔个人生平那种阴暗而强烈的牵引力。把被火药灼烧过的树干放在第二展厅陈列，使观众能够欣赏这些作品，而无需先被告知她在从罗马返回纽约后不久发生的遭遇所笼罩。事实证明，这些被焚烧的树干正是门迪塔在突然、暴力去世前创作的最后一批重要作品。

paragraphs.en: Late in the summer of 1985, Mendieta began packing up her studio at the American Academy, readying the space for its next resident. But she intended to stay in Rome, in an apartment in a residential neighborhood not far from the Forum. The place had been rented by her husband of a few months, the minimalist American sculptor Carl Andre, who had been shuttling between Berlin and New York for much of the period that Mendieta was in Italy.
paragraphs.zh: 1985年夏末，门迪塔开始清理自己在美国学院（American Academy）的工作室，为下一位驻留艺术家腾出空间。然而她打算留在罗马，住在离广场（Forum）不远的一个居民区公寓里。这个住处是她丈夫租下的，他是极简主义美国雕塑家卡尔·安德烈（Carl Andre）。在门迪塔身处意大利期间，他大多时间都在柏林和纽约之间往返。

paragraphs.en: Andre was among the preëminent artists of his generation. His groundbreaking work with industrial materials, including bricks and timber arranged into geometric forms, thrilled critics despite sometimes baffling the uninitiated. The dynamic between Andre and Mendieta was charged and often fractious. Friends remarked on their tendency to argue, particularly under the influence of alcohol. In the introduction to a 2011 book about Mendieta, the critic Lucy Lippard, a friend of both artists, wrote, “Her volatile alliance with Carl Andre, an old friend and political comrade of mine, was surprising, but what the hell, it seemed to work.” Their wedding, held in Rome, had been a gesture of romance and optimism. And they had a fruitful aesthetic dialogue, evident in a collaboration on a limited-edition book of twenty lithographs, half of which featured images of leaves, made by Mendieta, and the other half outlines of rocks, made by Andre. But none of this could mask an underlying distrust—among other things, Mendieta believed that Andre, who had been married twice before, was serially unfaithful.
paragraphs.zh: 安德烈是他这一代最杰出的艺术家之一。他用砖块和木材拼出几何形态的开创性创作曾令评论界为之欢呼，尽管这些作品有时会让外行人摸不着头绪。安德烈与门迪塔之间的互动紧张而常常针锋相对。朋友们都提到他们特别喜欢争执，尤其是在酒精作用下。2011年关于门迪塔的一本书的序言中，评论家兼两位艺术家共同的朋友露西·利帕德写道：“她与卡尔·安德烈这段多变的联盟，令人惊讶，但管他呢，似乎确实起了作用。” 他们在罗马举行的婚礼带有浪漫和乐观的意味，也确实孕育了富有成效的艺术对话，这在一本限量版石版画集上表现得很明显：全书二十幅版画中，有一半是门迪塔创作的叶片影像，另一半是安德烈创作的岩石轮廓。然而，即使如此，也掩盖不了双方内在的互不信任——尤其是门迪塔相信安德烈（他此前已经结过两次婚）是反复不忠的。

paragraphs.en: Before Mendieta could fully settle into the new apartment in Rome, she had to fly back to New York City to take care of some business. Andre would be there, too. About a week after she flew to America, in the early hours of September 8th, she plummeted to her death from a window of Andre’s apartment, on the thirty-fourth floor of a high-rise on Mercer Street, in Greenwich Village. Andre, who was also at the apartment, was charged with her murder. Nearly two and a half years later, he was acquitted.
paragraphs.zh: 在门迪塔还没彻底安顿好罗马的新公寓之前，她必须飞回纽约处理一些事务，安德烈也会在那里。她飞往美国大约一周后，9月8日清晨，她从格林威治村美塞尔街上一栋高层公寓的三十四层窗口坠落身亡。那天安德烈也在公寓里，他被控谋杀门迪塔。两年半后，他被宣告无罪。

paragraphs.en: There will undoubtedly be museumgoers at the Tate Modern whose introduction to the artist was not through her work but through a retelling of the circumstances of her death. At the time, the details were voyeuristically chronicled by the press. In December, 1985, New York ran the headline “Did Carl Andre, the Renowned Minimalist Sculptor, Hurl His Wife, a Fellow Artist, to Her Death?” The magazine’s cover featured a large portrait of Andre alongside a small snapshot of Mendieta. She went unnamed.
paragraphs.zh: 无疑，在泰特现代美术馆会有不少观众，他们对这位艺术家的第一印象并非来自她的作品，而是来自她死亡经过的反复讲述。那时，媒体对事件细节的报道近乎窥私般地铺陈开来。1985年12月，《纽约》杂志刊出头条： “著名极简主义雕塑家卡尔·安德烈是否将其妻子、同样是艺术家的一人，推下高空？” 该杂志封面是安德烈的大幅画像，配以门迪塔的一张小照片。文章中并未点名她。

paragraphs.en: In 1990, the journalist Robert Katz published “Naked by the Window,” a comprehensive account of the trial, which he had attended. Andre’s lawyer, Jack S. Hoffinger, had declared that the artist would take the surprising path of forgoing a jury in favor of a bench trial, in which the judge alone makes a ruling, and argued that Mendieta’s death had been either an accident or a “subintentional suicide” under the influence of alcohol. Mendieta’s friends and family thought it was preposterous to paint her as suicidal, given the many plans she had made for the future. Mendieta had even told intimates that she was gathering documentary evidence of Andre’s infidelity in advance of demanding a divorce, but, to their dismay, that information was ruled hearsay, and thus could not be admitted. The forensic evidence was murky, although a doorman told the police that he’d heard someone shouting “No!” and “Don’t!” “Naked by the Window”—the title is derived from an early poem by Andre—has a floridity influenced by Truman Capote. In a passage attributed in an annotation to an interview that the author conducted with the judge, Alvin Schlesinger, Katz suggests that Schlesinger was ambivalent about his not-guilty verdict: “Odd sort of person, Carl. He probably did it. Fifteen years was the least he would have to serve, if found guilty. Interesting case. Very close call.”
paragraphs.zh: 1990年，记者罗伯特·卡茨出版了《窗前裸影》，这是对他亲赴法庭旁听后写下的该案完整记录。安德烈的律师杰克·S·霍芬格尔宣布，安德烈会采取出人意料的策略，放弃陪审团改由法官单独裁决的庭审，并辩称门迪塔的死要么是事故，要么是在酒精影响下的“次要自杀”倾向所致。门迪塔的朋友和家人认为，将她描绘成有自杀倾向是荒唐的，因为她当时已经为未来制定了诸多计划。门迪塔甚至告诉亲近的人，她正在提前搜集安德烈不忠行为的书面证据，以便据此提出离婚；但遗憾的是，这些内容被认定为“传闻证据”，不能被采纳。法医证据并不清晰，尽管一名门卫向警方表示，他听见有人在大声喊“不要！”和“别！”“窗前裸影”这一标题取自安德烈早期的一首诗。书中一段注释声称来源于作者与法官阿尔文·施莱辛格的采访，卡茨在文中暗示施莱辛格对自己“无罪”裁决心存两难：“怪怪的家伙，卡尔。也许就是他干的。若定罪，最少得判十五年。很有意思的案子。非常接近（边界的）。”

paragraphs.en: “Naked by the Window” is out of print, but the elevation of Mendieta’s posthumous reputation has given it renewed currency. The book is being adapted by the actress America Ferrera for a TV series, in which Ferrera will star. The ceaseless appetite for true-crime narratives has also driven interest in Mendieta, and “Naked by the Window” informed a 2022 season of the podcast “Death of an Artist,” in which Helen Molesworth, a former curator at the Museum of Contemporary Art in Los Angeles, considered the life and work of both Mendieta and Andre. The podcast, which drew on Katz’s recorded interviews, was thoughtful and respectful, with due consideration given by Molesworth to Mendieta’s artistic career. At the same time, it fit the parameters of the true-crime genre, with Molesworth and her producer, Maria Luisa Tucker, trying to corner Andre at the apartment building on Mercer Street, where he continued to live for decades after Mendieta’s death. Molesworth’s discomfort is palpable: “Apparently, when journalists hit brick walls with e-mail and phone messages, they do something called doorstepping, which is when you literally just show up at someone’s doorstep. Needless to say, they don’t teach this technique in art-history class.” (Molesworth never managed to speak with Andre, who died in 2024.)
paragraphs.zh: 《窗前裸身》（《Naked by the Window》）已经停印，但门迪埃身后在死后持续上升的名声使这本书重获关注。该书正由演员阿梅莉亚·费雷拉改编为电视剧，由她主演。对真实犯罪叙事的持续热情也推动了人们对门迪埃的兴趣，《窗前裸身》为2022年的播客《艺术家之死》（《Death of an Artist》）一季提供了素材，该节目中，洛杉矶当代艺术博物馆前馆长海伦·莫莱斯沃思讨论了门迪埃与安德烈两人的生平与创作。该播客借用了卡茨的录音访谈，内容周到且尊重人文，在莫莱斯沃思对门迪埃艺术生涯的回应上有着充分的考量。与此同时，它也符合真实犯罪题材的叙事框架，莫莱斯沃思和制片人玛丽亚·路易莎·塔克（Maria Luisa Tucker）试图在梅塞尔街（Mercer Street）的公寓楼前将安德烈“堵”住，因为他在门迪埃去世后在那栋楼里住了几十年。莫莱斯沃思明显感到不安：“显然，当记者在电子邮件和电话里碰壁时，他们会用一种叫做‘上门追访’的办法，也就是你就直接出现在某人家门口。毫无疑问，这些技巧在艺术史课上不会教授。”（莫莱斯沃思始终没能与安德烈对话，安德烈于2024年去世。）

paragraphs.en: Mendieta’s story has repeatedly been told outside the confines of galleries and museums. In a graphic novel by Christine Redfern and Caro Caron, “Who Is Ana Mendieta?,” published in 2011, the artist’s lifeless body is shown crumpled on the roof of a deli beneath Andre’s apartment. A caption notes, “Eerie echo of a 1973 ‘Silueta.’ ” A 2024 novel by Xochitl Gonzalez, “Anita de Monte Laughs Last,” which lightly fictionalizes Mendieta’s story, is also set for a screen adaptation, to be directed by Eva Longoria.
paragraphs.zh: 门迪埃的故事屡次在画廊和博物馆之外被讲述。2011年，克里斯汀·雷德芬（Christine Redfern）和卡罗·卡隆（Caro Caron）推出图像小说《谁是安娜·门迪埃？》（《Who Is Ana Mendieta?》），其中艺术家的尸体被描绘成倒在三明治店屋顶、正下方是安德烈公寓下方。配文写道：“1973年《剪影》（《Silueta》）的诡异回声。”Xochitl Gonzalez 于2024年写的小说《阿妮塔·德·蒙特最后一笑》（《Anita de Monte Laughs Last》）也对门迪埃的故事作了轻度虚构改写，并将拍成电影，由伊娃·隆戈莉亚（Eva Longoria）执导。

paragraphs.en: Mendieta’s life and legacy have been chronicled in several documentary shorts made by Raquel Cecilia Mendieta, who is at work on a feature-length documentary that will, she hopes, provide a counternarrative to unauthorized treatments. “I would like to tell her story holistically and honestly,” she told me. She has also written a script for a filmed dramatization of Mendieta’s life. “It’s from her point of view—she is telling us her story, so it’s all through her eyes, like a memory,” she said. Raquel explained that her ambition is to present Mendieta’s work the way the artist would have wanted it to be seen—which includes not dwelling on the circumstances of her death. “That’s a second of her life, right?” Raquel said. “It’s a blip. She shouldn’t be defined by something that happened to her. She should be defined by what she did.”
paragraphs.zh: 门迪埃的生平与遗产已在数个纪录短片中被记录，这些纪录片由瑞克尔·塞西莉亚·门迪埃（Raquel Cecilia Mendieta）制作，她正在制作一部长篇纪录片，希望提供对未经授权解读的反叙事。她对我说：“我想全面而诚实地讲述她的故事。”她还为一部戏剧化电影改编写好了剧本。“这是从她的视角出发——她在讲述自己的故事，所以全部从她的眼睛看出来，”她说。瑞克尔解释说，她的目标是让观众看到门迪埃希望自己被看到的样子——这包括不要执着于她死亡的环境。“那只是她生命中的一秒，对吗？”瑞克尔说。“那只是一个短暂插曲。她不该被某个发生在她身上的事件定义。她应该被她所做的事定义。”

paragraphs.en: Although the Tate Modern exhibition will focus on Mendieta’s art, Andre will be named, and the show will include a copy of “Pietre Foglie,” the leaf-and-stone lithograph book the couple collaborated on. Umansky, the curator, explained that it demonstrates a creative tension between the two artists. “There are the very organic and rounded shapes of Ana, versus the very squarish shapes of Carl, and it says so much about the artistic and intellectual fights that they had, and how this confrontation is interesting,” she said. “Ana clearly stood up to him throughout their relationship.” An effective retrospective, Umansky went on, necessarily includes an account of the artist’s life and practice, “and everything that feeds into it is legitimate.”
paragraphs.zh: 尽管泰特现代美术馆（Tate Modern）的展览将重点放在门迪埃的艺术上，但安德烈仍会被提及，展览也将展出《Pietre Foglie》——这本由两人合作完成、以叶片和石头为主题的石版画书。策展人乌曼斯基（Umansky）解释说，这本书展示了两位艺术家之间的创造性张力。“其中有非常有机、圆润的安娜形态，也有非常方正的卡尔形态，这说明了他们在艺术和思想上的斗争，以及这种对峙为何令人着迷，”她说。“安娜在整个关系中显然始终与他对立。”乌曼斯基接着说，一个有效的回顾展必须包含对艺术家生平与实践的叙述，“而一切与之相关的内容都是合理的。”

paragraphs.en: Is it ever possible to separate an appreciation of an artist’s work from the facts of his or her life? This question has been much debated in recent years, particularly in the wake of the #MeToo movement, during which many revered artists were exposed as, at the very least, deeply problematic men. One of Molesworth’s questions in her podcast was whether, after delving into Mendieta’s death, she could continue to admire Andre’s work. She concluded that, in her capacity as a museum curator, she could no longer bring herself to display it. But that didn’t mean she wanted to erase Andre, and she could still acknowledge his importance in art history. Spending time thinking about Mendieta and Andre “helped me realize that I wanted to be a member of a community that discusses grave and difficult things,” Molesworth said in her concluding episode. “It’s clear that our geniuses can do horrible things. It’s clear that talent and violence can exist in the same person.”
paragraphs.zh: 我们是否可能将对一位艺术家的作品欣赏与其人生事实彻底分开？近年来这一问题被反复讨论，尤其是在 #MeToo 运动之后，许多备受尊崇的艺术家被揭露至少是“问题重重”之人。莫尔斯沃斯在播客中的一个问题是，在深入追问门迪埃的死亡之后，她是否还能继续欣赏安德烈的作品。她在结论中说，作为一名博物馆馆长，她已无法再让自己去展出这些作品，但这并不意味着她想抹去安德烈，也不妨碍她仍然承认他在艺术史中的重要性。莫尔斯沃斯在最后一集里说：“花时间去思考门迪埃和安德烈帮助我意识到，我想成为一个讨论严肃而困难问题的社群的一员。很明显，我们的天才也会做出可怕的事。很明显，才华与暴力可以同时存在于同一个人身上。”

paragraphs.en: In the case of Mendieta, the question is a slightly different one: Is it possible to separate an appreciation of her work from the brutal spectacle of her death? This is particularly charged by the fact that, during the trial, Andre’s lawyer defended his client by trivializing Mendieta’s art, characterizing her creative themes as mere symptoms of a ghoulish obsession with death. In an outrageous line of questioning, documented by Katz, Hoffinger quizzed one witness about the theatrical aspect of Mendieta’s work, implying that, consciously or not, the artist’s demise was just another performance: “Do you know the art where she used her own body to make impressions on the ground?... And the work with blood running down her face?... Was Ana interested in the wedding of the human body with the earth?” Mendieta once told an interviewer that her interest in making impressions of her body on earth could be traced to her Cuban childhood, where she learned to crawl and walk on coastal sand. It would have served Andre’s lawyer less well to suggest that what inspired Mendieta’s practice was, quite literally, a day at the beach.
paragraphs.zh: 在门迪埃的案例中，问题稍有不同：是否可能将对她作品的欣赏与她那残酷的死亡景象分开？这一点尤其受到法庭审判时的影响，当时安德烈的律师为其辩护时，曾淡化门迪埃的艺术，将她的创作主题解释为对死亡有着病态着迷的“症状”。根据凯茨的记录，霍芬杰在一段离谱的问询中，让一位证人回答有关门迪埃作品戏剧化一面的提问，暗示不管有意还是无意，艺术家的陨落似乎只是另一场“表演”： “你知道她用自己的身体在地面上留下印痕的那件作品吗？……还有她脸上流着血的那组作品？……安娜是否对人体与大地的结合感兴趣？” 门迪埃曾对一位采访者说，她对在土地上留下身体印迹的兴趣可以追溯到在古巴的童年，那时她学会了在海岸沙地上爬行和行走。对安德烈的律师来说，若提出她的实践灵感只是“字面上”来自一次海边散步，显然站不住脚。

paragraphs.en: Given this historical context—and given how the tragic early deaths of female artists, from Sylvia Plath to Francesca Woodman to Marilyn Monroe to Amy Winehouse, are often framed in ways that diminish their accomplishments—it’s understandable that the custodians of Mendieta’s legacy have sought to discourage readings of her work that begin with her life’s end. Months in advance of the Tate Modern show, the Mendieta estate issued a preëmptive strike, in the form of a document titled “Fact Sheet.” It stated, “Ana Mendieta’s death does not define her, or her art. Her tragic death is often used as a means for sensationalism and clickbait, but it is not remotely related to how she created her art, nor was her art predestined or shaped by her death in any way, shape or form.” In this document, you can sense an anticipatory weariness at the inevitable regurgitation of gory details. In an interview two years ago with the Times, Raquel Cecilia Mendieta asked, “How many times does she have to fall?”
paragraphs.zh: 考虑到这一历史背景，以及从西尔维娅·普拉斯到弗朗西斯卡·伍德曼，再到玛丽莲·梦露、阿米·怀恩豪斯等女性艺术家早逝的悲剧常常被叙述得削弱她们成就的方式，也就不难理解，门迪埃遗产的管理者为何一直努力阻止把她的作品阅读成从她生命终点出发的解读。在泰特现代美术馆展览开幕前数月，门迪埃遗产发布了一份名为《事实清单》的文件，先发制人地反驳了这种做法。文件写道：“安娜·门迪埃的死亡不能定义她，也不能定义她的艺术。她的悲剧性死亡常被当作制造轰动和点击诱饵的手段，但这与她如何创作艺术并无任何关联，她的艺术也绝非以任何方式被她的死亡预设或塑造。” 在这份文件里，你几乎能感受到对必然反复被复述的血腥细节的先发式厌倦。两年前在《时代报》的一次采访中，拉克尔·塞西莉亚·门迪埃问道：“她到底得被提起多少次？”

paragraphs.en: Even among those who cherish Mendieta’s art, there has been a tendency to present her as an emblem of the way women are abused by men—an approach that results in a similar foregrounding of the circumstances of her death. In the past dozen years, museum shows featuring Andre’s work at Dia Beacon, at MOCA, and at the Tate Modern have drawn protesters holding posters reading “Where is Ana Mendieta?” The implication is that the ongoing veneration of Andre’s art is an example of the establishment’s unregenerate prioritizing of the male and the white over the female and the brown. (Although Andre continued to have a successful career after the trial, his reputation was indelibly marked by it; when he died, the first line of his Times obituary referred to “the accusation that he played a role in the death” of Mendieta.)
paragraphs.zh: 即使是在珍视门迪埃艺术的人群中，也有一种倾向，把她塑造成“女性被男性虐待”的象征，而这种做法同样会把她死亡时的情境推到前台。在过去十年里，Dia Beacon、MOCA 以及泰特现代美术馆举办安德烈作品展时，都出现过举着“安娜·门迪埃在哪里？”海报的抗议者。其含义是，安德烈艺术的持续崇拜正是体制对男性与白人优先、对女性与有色人种贬置的一例。 （尽管安德烈在审判后仍延续成功职业生涯，但他的名誉已被这场审判牢牢烙上印记；当他去世时，他在《时代报》讣告中的第一行提到“他在门迪埃死亡中扮演角色的指控”）

paragraphs.en: For some admirers of Mendieta, such a framing characterizes her foremost as Andre’s victim. Coco Fusco, a Cuban American artist twelve years Mendieta’s junior, told me, “I am of the belief that he probably did throw her out the window, but at the same time I don’t think there’s anything to be gained in terms of understanding her work by making that the most important thing to talk about.” Fusco prefers to focus on, say, the links Mendieta forged between American and Cuban artists once she started returning to her homeland, in the early eighties, or the urgent ways in which Mendieta drew upon Afro-Cuban syncretic faiths to express herself. “I think it’s more relevant to think about it in terms of the relationship to her life experience, which was very traumatic in many ways, and in some ways emblematic,” Fusco said.
paragraphs.zh: 对一些门迪塔的崇拜者来说，这种说法首先将她界定为安德烈的受害者。古巴裔美国艺术家、比门迪塔年轻十二岁的科科·弗斯科对我说：“我相信他很可能把她扔出了窗外，但与此同时，我不认为把这件事当作谈论她作品最重要的内容会让我们更理解她的创作。”弗斯科更愿意关注的是，例如门迪塔在八十年代初重新回到故乡后，与美国和古巴艺术家建立的联系，或她如何迫切地借助古巴-非洲（阿弗洛-古巴）融合信仰来表达自己。弗斯科说：“我认为更相关的是从她的人生经历去理解她，而这种经历在许多方面都非常创伤性，而且在某种意义上是有代表性的。”

paragraphs.en: Of course, artists and their estates can’t control how a body of work will be received by viewers. And it would not serve Mendieta’s art to avert the critical gaze entirely from the ways her work does tackle the subject of death. After all, she did stage a fake murder, lie in a tomb, and repeatedly douse her body in blood. Throughout her lifetime, her work was seen as a meditation on violence, especially against women, and it would likely have continued to be seen that way even if Mendieta had lived a long life with a peaceful end. The enduring attraction of “Body Tracks” lies in its suggestive openness to multiple interpretations. The work can be read as a commentary on the act of mark-making. It is equally reasonable for a viewer to look at the stains of blood dragged down the wall and shudder at what the work says about the frailty of the human body and its subjection to the irresistible force of gravity. “Imágen de Yágul,” the work in which Mendieta inserted her own body into a tomb and decked herself with flowers, was explicitly a way for the artist to tie herself to the ancients and to history—to the living and the dead, to death and rebirth, to the common fate of every body. Mortality was among Mendieta’s subjects, just as water, fire, earth, and air were among her materials.
paragraphs.zh: 当然，艺术家本人及其遗产无法控制观众如何接收某一作品体系。也不该因为这一点就完全回避门迪塔作品中处理死亡主题的方式。毕竟，她确实表演过一场“假谋杀”、躺过墓穴，还反复用血液浸湿自己的身体。她一生的作品都被视为对暴力——尤其是针对女性的暴力——的一种冥想；即使门迪塔若能长寿并安然离世，她的作品很可能仍会被这样理解。《身体轨迹》之所以持久吸引观众，正因为它对多重诠释敞开着。作品既可被解读为对“留痕行为”的评论；同样，观者完全可以看着血迹从墙面向下拖拽而下并因此感到一阵战栗，意识到这件作品在说人类身体的脆弱，以及其屈从于重力不可抗拒力量的处境。《Imágen de Yágul》这件作品中，门迪塔将自己的身体置入墓穴，并以鲜花装点自己，这是一种将自己与古人和历史相连的明确方式——与生者和逝者相连，与死亡与重生相连，与每一具身体共同的命运相连。死亡是门迪塔的主题之一，正如水、火、土和空气一样，也是她的材料之一。

paragraphs.en: “I don’t think that you can separate death and life,” Mendieta told Linda Montano, an American performance artist, in an interview published in the late eighties. “All of my work is about those two things—it’s about Eros and life and death.” Following Mendieta’s demise, Ida Panicelli, her curator friend in Rome, went to her studio at the American Academy to assess Mendieta’s belongings. Amid packed boxes, she found an experiment under way, in which Mendieta had encased part of a prickly-pear cactus in wax. The encasement was humid, and a single bud had pushed its way through the waxen tomb and into the light of the studio. “I said, ‘My God, she’s here,’ ” Panicelli told me. “It was like feeling her presence—still there, nurturing a sculpture.” Like the best of Mendieta’s tragically truncated body of work, this unfinished piece had a fragility and an ephemerality, as well as a resilient fortitude. Panicelli added, “It was something very close to death in the work, and incredibly close to life.” ♦
paragraphs.zh: “我想你不能把死亡和生命分开，”门迪塔在一次八十年代末出版的采访中对美国行为艺术家琳达·蒙塔诺说，“我所有的作品都围绕这两件事——它们关于爱欲、生命和死亡。”门迪塔去世后，她在罗马的策展人朋友伊达·帕尼切利前往美国学院，去清点门迪塔留下的物品。在堆满箱子的房间里，她发现一项实验正在进行中：门迪塔将仙人掌（仙人掌属植物中常见的仙人掌属，俗称仙人掌中的“仙人掌果”）的一部分包埋在蜡中。包埋体潮湿，而一枚花苞已经穿过蜡制“墓体”向室内光线中探出头来。帕尼切利对我说：“我说，‘我的天啊，她还在这儿。’” “这就像感到她的在场——仍在这里，滋养着一件雕塑。” 正如门迪塔那部痛失完整却最为杰出的身体艺术作品中最好的部分，这件未完成作品既有脆弱与短暂，也有顽强的坚韧。帕尼切利补充说：“在这件作品里，离死亡非常近，同时也非常接近生命。” ♦
